title: Carved ash cooking spoon
subtitle: Green-wood axe-to-knife spoon for the stovetop
excerpt: A long-handled cooking spoon in green ash, shaped axe-to-knife from a riven billet. Sycamore is the classic spoon wood, but ash carves cleanly green and dries food-safe. This is an intermediate step from the eating spoon; longer handle, deeper bowl, heavier blank.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for the axe-and-knife blank-shaping progression; Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry for the riven-billet method.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly felled or recently riven timber, still high in moisture content. Carves easily under hand tools and can be shaped axe-to-knife before final knife finishing.
Split along the grain with a froe and maul, rather than sawn across it. A riven blank follows the grain perfectly and is stronger than a sawn equivalent.
The rough-shaped piece of wood from which the finished item is carved.
The safe default cut: blade travelling away from the body, driven by the forearm, cutting off the end of the workpiece into open air.
A curved-bladed knife for hollowing the bowl of a spoon or kuksa. Available in small, medium, and large radii.

content.text: A cooking spoon needs a longer handle than an eating spoon; 30 to 35 cm against the usual 20; and a deeper, wider bowl that can push food around a pan without losing it over the rim. The
blank
starts bigger, the axe work is heavier, and the
hook-knife
pass takes longer. This is the logical second spoon project after the eating spoon: the same method, scaled up and held to a tighter finish because a cooking spoon takes real daily use.
The project wants
green wood

. Ash carves cleanly under hand tools when freshly cut, dries food-safe pale, and its straight grain runs the length of the blank without wandering; which matters when the blank is 35 cm long and an errant grain line can split it on the axe. Sycamore is the traditional spoon wood and works equally well here. Birch, lime, and cherry are all usable.
The wood
Source a freshly
riven

ash billet: 38 cm long, 10 cm across the widest face, 6 cm deep. The billet should show a slight gloss on the freshly cut end and carve without the shavings curling brown; signs it is properly green. Coppice off-cuts, windfall ash, and tree-surgeon's skip material all work. Ash is plentiful across the UK; the fungal disease ash dieback has left many councils with felled timber available free to collectors.

Ash dries quickly compared to sycamore. Work the spoon to near-finished shape in one session and let it dry slowly away from direct heat. If the blank cracks before you reach the hook-knife stage, the walls are too thick; re-rough thinner.
Tools

You need a carving axe, a sloyd knife, a large hook knife for the wide bowl, a leather strop, and raw linseed oil for finishing. A side-axe block; a heavy end-grain log at knee height; is useful for the initial roughing but not essential if you can work on a stump or low bench. Every edge must take a hair off the back of your forearm before you pick up the blank. A hook knife that drags rather than cuts will not hollow the bowl cleanly.
Method
Keep a
push cut
as your default throughout: blade travelling away from the body, forearm driving forward, cut finishing off the end of the workpiece into open air. Strop every 15 minutes of carving.

Draw the spoon profile on the flat face of the billet in pencil: handle line, neck, bowl oval. The handle should be 25 cm from the back of the bowl to the end; the bowl oval 8 cm long and 6 cm wide. Mark the bowl depth on the side face; the bowl floor sits 2 cm below the flat face.

Rough the profile with the carving axe, cutting to within 5 mm of the pencil lines. Work with the grain; on an ash blank this means cutting from the handle end toward the bowl on one pass and from the bowl toward the handle end on the return, reading which way the grain runs with each axe stroke. A stroke against the grain will dive and catch rather than lift cleanly.

Refine the handle profile with the sloyd knife using push cuts along the handle and thumb-pivot cuts at the neck. The handle cross-section should be oval, not round; flatter on the palm side than the back. Four-finger grip on the blank; thumb-push grip on the knife. The handle is finished when the neck flows into the bowl without a step.

Hollow the bowl with the large hook knife. Grip: the bowl face toward you, hook knife in the cutting hand, thumb of the holding hand braced against the back of the hook-knife blade to control the arc. Take shavings working from the rim toward the centre; not the other way. Cutting toward the centre goes with the grain; cutting from the centre outward often goes against it and tears.

Check the bowl depth by holding the spoon to the light and looking at the wall thickness from the back. The walls should be 4 to 5 mm at the rim and no less than 3 mm at the centre. A wall that shows light through it is too thin and will crack as the blank dries. A wall that looks opaque and heavy is still too thick; keep hollowing.

Refine the back of the bowl with push cuts and thumb-pivot cuts on the sloyd knife. The outside of the bowl should be convex and smooth, narrowing toward the neck without a sharp transition. The grain runs roughly along the length of the spoon; cuts that go from bowl to neck travel with the grain. Cuts from neck to bowl often go against it; test with a light stroke before committing.

Dry the spoon slowly. Lay it flat on a shelf away from radiators and direct sun. Check it daily for the first week. A crack appearing in the neck or bowl means the blank dried unevenly; usually because the walls are too thick on one side. If you catch it early (a hairline, not a split), the crack often closes as moisture equalises.
Finishing

Once the spoon has dried; 2 to 3 weeks for an ash spoon in a normally heated room; sand through 120 and 180 grit, following the grain. Apply raw linseed oil liberally, let it soak in for an hour, then wipe off the surplus with a clean cloth. Lay the oiled cloth flat to dry before disposing; do not bundle it, as oil-soaked rags can smoulder. Raw linseed oil takes 2 weeks to cure fully before the spoon is food-safe. Re-oil after the first five or six washes.
Variations
Sycamore: the same method, slightly lighter grain. The preferred wood where ash is not available fresh.
Left-handed version: the bowl hollowing follows the same grain direction but the bowl is angled left at the neck for a left-handed hold.
Flat-bottomed variant: hollow the bowl less deeply; 1 cm rather than 2; to make a roux spoon that sits flat on the pan floor.
Care

Wash promptly after use with warm water and a small amount of washing-up liquid. Towel-dry immediately; never leave it soaking in water or put it through the dishwasher. Re-oil when the wood looks dull and dry, roughly monthly in regular use.

items.symptom: The blank splits along the neck during drying
items.cause: Wall thickness uneven; one side thicker than the other, creating stress as moisture leaves at different rates
items.fix: Carve neck walls to an even 4 mm all around before drying. A neck that looks finished at 6 mm on one face and 3 mm on the other will crack. Sand or re-carve to equalise before leaving to dry.

items.symptom: Hook knife skips across the surface without cutting
items.cause: Edge is dull; the hook knife dulls faster than the sloyd knife
items.fix: Strop the hook knife on the concave side of the leather strop, pulling the edge along the strop rather than pushing it. Three passes on the strop often restore the bite.

items.symptom: Axe strokes dive into the grain rather than lifting chips cleanly
items.fix: Read the grain before each stroke: the grain lines should run toward the edge you are cutting toward. If a stroke tears rather than lifts, reverse direction.

items.symptom: Bowl walls are translucent at the rim after hollowing
items.cause: Hook knife has gone past the 3 mm safe wall thickness
items.fix: Stop hollowing the rim. Deepen the centre of the bowl instead, which thickens the rim relative to the bowl floor. Accept a slightly shallower bowl or start a new blank.
